category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 4 - Probability and Statistics/Probability Theory

questiontext.text: Bayes' theorem states that for events \(A\) and \(B\) with \(P(B) > 0\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(P(A|B) = \dfrac{P(B|A)P(A)}{P(B)}\)

Bayes' theorem relates the conditional probability \(P(A|B)\) to \(P(B|A)\): \(P(A|B) = \frac{P(B|A)P(A)}{P(B)}\). Here, \(P(A)\) is the prior probability, \(P(B|A)\) is the likelihood, and \(P(A|B)\) is the posterior probability. By the law of total probability: \(P(B) = P(B|A)P(A) + P(B|A^c)P(A^c)\). Bayes' theorem is the foundation of Bayesian inference.

questiontext.text: Two events \(A\) and \(B\) are independent if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)\)

Events \(A\) and \(B\) are (stochastically) independent iff \(P(A \cap B) = P(A)P(B)\). Equivalently (when \(P(B) > 0\)), \(P(A|B) = P(A)\) — knowing \(B\) occurred gives no information about \(A\). Note: independence is different from mutual exclusivity. Mutually exclusive events (with positive probability) are actually negatively correlated (dependent).

questiontext.text: Chebyshev's inequality states that for a random variable \(X\) with mean \(\mu\) and variance \(\sigma^2\), and any \(k > 0\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(P(|X - \mu| \geq k\sigma) \leq \dfrac{1}{k^2}\)

Chebyshev's inequality: \(P(|X-\mu| \geq k\sigma) \leq 1/k^2\). This holds for any distribution with finite variance — no knowledge of the distribution shape is required. For \(k=2\): at least 75% of values lie within 2 standard deviations; for \(k=3\): at least 89%. Chebyshev's inequality is used to prove the Weak Law of Large Numbers.

questiontext.text: The Central Limit Theorem states that if \(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n\) are i.i.d. with mean \(\mu\) and variance \(\sigma^2\), then as \(n \to \infty\), the distribution of \(\dfrac{\bar{X}_n - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}\) converges to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Standard normal distribution \(N(0,1)\)

The Central Limit Theorem (CLT): \(\frac{\bar{X}_n - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \xrightarrow{d} N(0,1)\) as \(n \to \infty\), regardless of the distribution of \(X_i\) (provided \(\mu\) and \(\sigma^2\) are finite). This explains why the normal distribution appears so frequently in statistics. The CLT is the theoretical justification for using normal-based inference for large samples.

questiontext.text: The Weak Law of Large Numbers states that for i.i.d. random variables \(X_1, X_2, \ldots\) with mean \(\mu\), as \(n \to \infty\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\bar{X}_n \xrightarrow{P} \mu\) (converges in probability)

WLLN: \(\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i \xrightarrow{P} \mu\), i.e., for any \(\epsilon > 0\), \(P(|\bar{X}_n - \mu| > \epsilon) \to 0\). The Strong LLN gives almost sure convergence: \(\bar{X}_n \xrightarrow{a.s.} \mu\). The WLLN requires finite variance (Chebyshev's proof), but can be proved with only finite mean (Kolmogorov's proof). This justifies using the sample mean as an estimate of the population mean.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 4 - Probability and Statistics/Probability Distributions

questiontext.text: If \(X \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)\), then the mean and variance of \(X\) are respectively:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Both equal to \(\lambda\)

For \(X \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)\): \(P(X=k) = e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k/k!\) for \(k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots\). The mean \(E[X] = \lambda\) and variance \(\text{Var}(X) = \lambda\). This equal mean-variance property is a defining characteristic of the Poisson distribution. It arises as a limit of the Binomial distribution when \(n \to \infty\), \(p \to 0\), and \(np \to \lambda\).

questiontext.text: If \(X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)\), then \(P(\mu - 2\sigma < X < \mu + 2\sigma)\) is approximately:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) 0.9545 (approximately 95%)

The empirical rule (68-95-99.7 rule) for normal distributions:
• \(P(\mu - \sigma < X < \mu + \sigma) \approx 0.6827\) (68.27%)
• \(P(\mu - 2\sigma < X < \mu + 2\sigma) \approx 0.9545\) (95.45%)
• \(P(\mu - 3\sigma < X < \mu + 3\sigma) \approx 0.9973\) (99.73%)
This follows from \(P(-2 < Z < 2) = 2\Phi(2) - 1 \approx 2(0.9772) - 1 = 0.9544\).

questiontext.text: The exponential distribution \(\text{Exp}(\lambda)\) has the memoryless property, which means:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)\)

The memoryless property: \(P(X > s+t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)\) for all \(s, t > 0\). This means "given that the event hasn't occurred in the first \(s\) time units, the probability that it takes at least \(t\) more time units is the same as if we started fresh." The exponential distribution is the only continuous distribution with this property; the geometric distribution is its discrete analogue. It is used to model inter-arrival times in Poisson processes.

answer.text: \(P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)\)
\(P(X > s + t) = P(X > s) \cdot P(X > t)\)
The distribution does not depend on \(\lambda\)

questiontext.text: If \(X \sim B(n, p)\), the variance of \(X\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(np(1-p)\)

For \(X \sim B(n,p)\): \(P(X=k) = \binom{n}{k}p^k(1-p)^{n-k}\). The mean is \(E[X] = np\) and variance is \(\text{Var}(X) = np(1-p) = npq\) where \(q = 1-p\). The variance is maximized at \(p = 1/2\), giving \(\text{Var} = n/4\). The Binomial can be approximated by \(N(np, np(1-p))\) for large \(n\) (CLT) or by \(\text{Poisson}(np)\) when \(p\) is small.

questiontext.text: If \(Z_1, Z_2, \ldots, Z_n\) are i.i.d. \(N(0,1)\), then \(\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^n Z_i^2\) follows a:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Chi-squared distribution with \(n\) degrees of freedom

\(\sum_{i=1}^n Z_i^2 \sim \chi^2(n)\). The chi-squared distribution with \(n\) df has mean \(n\) and variance \(2n\). It is used in: testing goodness of fit (\(\chi^2\) test), testing independence, confidence intervals for variance (\(s^2(n-1)/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2(n-1)\)). The sum of independent chi-squared variables is chi-squared with degrees of freedom equal to the sum.

answer.text: Chi-squared distribution with \(n\) degrees of freedom

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 4 - Probability and Statistics/Statistical Inference

questiontext.text: The Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE) of the parameter \(p\) in a Binomial distribution \(B(n, p)\), based on observing \(x\) successes in \(n\) trials, is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\hat{p} = x/n\)

The likelihood is \(L(p) = \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x}\). Taking the log and differentiating: \(\frac{d\ln L}{dp} = \frac{x}{p} - \frac{n-x}{1-p} = 0\), giving \(\hat{p} = x/n\) (the sample proportion). The MLE is unbiased (\(E[\hat{p}] = p\)) and efficient (achieves the Cramér-Rao lower bound). MLEs are invariant under reparametrization.

questiontext.text: An estimator \(\hat{\theta}\) of a parameter \(\theta\) is said to be unbiased if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta\)

An estimator is unbiased if its expected value equals the true parameter. The bias is \(B(\hat{\theta}) = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta\). For example, the sample mean \(\bar{X}\) is an unbiased estimator of the population mean \(\mu\), and \(S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i-\bar{X})^2\) is unbiased for \(\sigma^2\) (note the \(n-1\) denominator). The MSE = Variance + Bias²; for unbiased estimators, MSE = Variance.

questiontext.text: The Cramér-Rao lower bound states that for any unbiased estimator \(\hat{\theta}\) of \(\theta\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \dfrac{1}{I(\theta)}\)

The Cramér-Rao inequality: \(\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{nI(\theta)}\) where \(I(\theta) = E\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial \ln f(X;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right)^2\right]\) is the Fisher information. An estimator achieving this bound is called efficient (or UMVUE). MLEs are asymptotically efficient. The bound cannot be achieved for all distributions; when it can, the distribution belongs to the exponential family.

questiontext.text: A 95% confidence interval for the population mean \(\mu\), based on a sample of size \(n\) with sample mean \(\bar{x}\) and known population standard deviation \(\sigma\), is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\left(\bar{x} - 1.96\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}},\; \bar{x} + 1.96\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right)\)

The 95% CI for \(\mu\) when \(\sigma\) is known: \(\bar{x} \pm z_{0.025} \cdot \sigma/\sqrt{n} = \bar{x} \pm 1.96 \sigma/\sqrt{n}\), since \(z_{0.025} = 1.96\) (the 97.5th percentile of \(N(0,1)\)). Interpretation: if we repeat the sampling many times, 95% of such intervals will contain the true \(\mu\). When \(\sigma\) is unknown, replace \(\sigma\) by \(s\) and \(1.96\) by \(t_{n-1,0.025}\).

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 4 - Probability and Statistics/Testing of Hypotheses

name.text: Hypothesis Testing - Type I and Type II Errors

questiontext.text: In hypothesis testing, a Type I error occurs when:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) We reject \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is actually true

Types of errors in hypothesis testing:
• Type I error (False Positive): Reject \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is true. Probability = \(\alpha\) (significance level).
• Type II error (False Negative): Fail to reject \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is false. Probability = \(\beta\).
• Power = \(1 - \beta\) = probability of correctly rejecting a false \(H_0\).
There is a trade-off: decreasing \(\alpha\) (Type I error) increases \(\beta\) (Type II error) for fixed sample size.

answer.text: We reject \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is actually true
We fail to reject \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is false

questiontext.text: The Neyman-Pearson lemma states that the most powerful test of size \(\alpha\) for testing a simple null \(H_0: \theta = \theta_0\) against a simple alternative \(H_1: \theta = \theta_1\) is the:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Likelihood ratio test that rejects \(H_0\) when \(L(\theta_1)/L(\theta_0) > k\)

Neyman-Pearson Lemma: the most powerful test of size \(\alpha\) rejects \(H_0\) when the likelihood ratio \(\Lambda = L(\theta_1)/L(\theta_0) > k_\alpha\), where \(k_\alpha\) is chosen so that \(P(\Lambda > k_\alpha \mid H_0) = \alpha\). This is the uniformly most powerful (UMP) test for simple vs. simple hypotheses. For one-sided composite alternatives in exponential families, UMP tests exist via the monotone likelihood ratio property.

question.single: truetrueabc Test that maximizes the Type II error Likelihood ratio test that rejects \(H_0\) when \(L(\theta_1)/L(\theta_0) > k\) Test based on the sample mean only Test that minimizes the sample size

name.text: Hypothesis Testing - Chi-square Goodness of Fit

questiontext.text: In a chi-square goodness-of-fit test with \(k\) categories and no estimated parameters, the degrees of freedom are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(k - 1\)

The chi-square statistic \(\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^k \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}\) has approximately a \(\chi^2\) distribution with \(k-1\) degrees of freedom when no parameters are estimated (one constraint: \(\sum O_i = n\)). If \(m\) parameters are estimated from the data, the df becomes \(k - 1 - m\). This test compares observed frequencies \(O_i\) with expected frequencies \(E_i\) under \(H_0\).

questiontext.text: The \(p\)-value of a hypothesis test is defined as:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) The probability, under \(H_0\), of obtaining a test statistic at least as extreme as the observed one

The \(p\)-value = \(P(\text{observed statistic or more extreme} \mid H_0)\). We reject \(H_0\) at significance level \(\alpha\) iff \(p \leq \alpha\). A small \(p\)-value provides evidence against \(H_0\). Importantly, the \(p\)-value is NOT the probability that \(H_0\) is true — it is the probability of the data (or more extreme data) given \(H_0\).

answer.text: The probability, under \(H_0\), of a test statistic at least as extreme as the observed value

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 4 - Probability and Statistics/Markov Chains and Stochastic Processes

questiontext.text: A stochastic process \(\{X_n\}_{n \geq 0}\) is a Markov chain if it satisfies the Markov property:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i, X_{n-1}, \ldots, X_0) = P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i)\)

The Markov property ("memoryless" property for stochastic processes): the future state depends only on the present state, not on how we arrived there. This is the key property that makes Markov chains tractable. The transition probability \(p_{ij} = P(X_{n+1}=j \mid X_n=i)\) defines the transition matrix \(P = (p_{ij})\), where \(P^n\) gives n-step transition probabilities.

answer.text: \(P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i, X_{n-1}, \ldots, X_0) = P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i)\)
\(P(X_{n+1} = j) = P(X_n = i)\) for all \(i, j\)

questiontext.text: A probability distribution \(\pi\) is a stationary distribution of a Markov chain with transition matrix \(P\) if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\pi P = \pi\) (i.e., \(\sum_i \pi_i p_{ij} = \pi_j\) for all \(j\))

A stationary (invariant) distribution \(\pi\) satisfies \(\pi P = \pi\) (vector-matrix form) or equivalently \(\pi_j = \sum_i \pi_i p_{ij}\) for all states \(j\), with \(\sum_j \pi_j = 1\) and \(\pi_j \geq 0\). For irreducible, positive recurrent Markov chains, a unique stationary distribution exists and equals the long-run proportions of time spent in each state (ergodic theorem). For detailed balance (reversibility): \(\pi_i p_{ij} = \pi_j p_{ji}\).

answer.text: \(\pi P = \pi\) (i.e., \(\pi\) is a left eigenvector of \(P\) with eigenvalue 1)

questiontext.text: A state \(i\) of a Markov chain is said to be recurrent if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) The probability of returning to state \(i\) starting from \(i\) is 1

State \(i\) is recurrent if \(f_{ii} = P(\text{ever return to } i \mid X_0 = i) = 1\). It is transient if \(f_{ii} < 1\). Equivalently, \(i\) is recurrent iff \(\sum_{n=0}^\infty p_{ii}^{(n)} = \infty\) (the expected number of returns is infinite). A recurrent state is positive recurrent if the mean return time \(\mu_i = E[\text{return time to } i \mid X_0=i] < \infty\), and null recurrent if \(\mu_i = \infty\).

answer.text: The probability of returning to state \(i\) is 0
The chain stays in state \(i\) forever
The probability of returning to state \(i\) starting from \(i\) is 1
The mean return time to \(i\) is finite

questiontext.text: A Poisson process \(\{N(t)\}_{t \geq 0}\) with rate \(\lambda > 0\) has the property that the number of arrivals in a time interval of length \(t\) follows a:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Poisson distribution with mean \(\lambda t\)

Key properties of a Poisson process with rate \(\lambda\): (1) \(N(0)=0\); (2) Independent increments; (3) Stationary increments: \(N(t+s)-N(s) \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda t)\); (4) Inter-arrival times \(T_i \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)\) and are i.i.d. The Poisson process is the canonical model for random arrivals (radioactive decay, telephone calls, etc.) and is the basis for more general birth-death processes.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 4 - Probability and Statistics/Sampling Theory

questiontext.text: In simple random sampling (SRS) without replacement from a population of size \(N\) with mean \(\mu\) and variance \(\sigma^2\), the variance of the sample mean \(\bar{x}\) based on a sample of size \(n\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\dfrac{\sigma^2}{n} \cdot \dfrac{N-n}{N-1}\)

For SRS without replacement: \(\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \cdot \frac{N-n}{N-1}\). The factor \(\frac{N-n}{N-1}\) is the finite population correction (fpc). When \(n/N \to 0\) (small sampling fraction), fpc \(\approx 1\) and the formula reduces to the i.i.d. case \(\sigma^2/n\). The fpc reflects the reduction in variance due to sampling without replacement (we "know" more about the population as \(n \to N\)).

questiontext.text: In stratified random sampling with proportional allocation, the sample size from stratum \(h\) (of size \(N_h\)) is chosen as:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(n_h = n \cdot N_h / N\)

Proportional allocation: each stratum contributes to the sample in proportion to its size in the population, so \(n_h = n \cdot N_h/N\). This ensures the sample mirrors the population structure (self-weighting sample). Optimal (Neyman) allocation additionally considers within-stratum variability: \(n_h \propto N_h \sigma_h\), minimizing total variance for a given total sample size. Stratified sampling generally achieves lower variance than SRS when strata are homogeneous internally.

answer.text: \(n_h = n / H\) (equal allocation, \(H\) = number of strata)

questiontext.text: In systematic sampling from a population of \(N = 1000\) units with a sample of size \(n = 50\), we select every \(k\)-th unit where \(k\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) 20

The sampling interval is \(k = N/n = 1000/50 = 20\). In systematic sampling, we randomly select a starting point \(r\) from 1 to \(k\), then select units \(r, r+k, r+2k, \ldots\). This is simpler to implement than SRS and often more efficient when the population is randomly ordered. However, if there is periodicity in the population matching the sampling interval, the sample may be biased.

questiontext.text: Spearman's rank correlation coefficient \(r_s\) is calculated using the formula (where \(d_i\) is the difference in ranks and \(n\) is the number of pairs):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(r_s = 1 - \dfrac{6\sum d_i^2}{n(n^2-1)}\)

Spearman's rank correlation: \(r_s = 1 - \frac{6\sum d_i^2}{n(n^2-1)}\), where \(d_i = R(X_i) - R(Y_i)\) is the difference between the ranks of \(X_i\) and \(Y_i\). It ranges from \(-1\) (perfect negative ranking) to \(+1\) (perfect positive ranking). It is a non-parametric measure of monotone association, useful when data are ordinal or when the Pearson correlation's normality assumption is not met.
